reference: GATE CS — Linear Algebra / Matrices and Systems

map.condition: Decompose A into lower and upper triangular factors
Solve Ax = b after LU factorisation
Account for row swaps during Gaussian elimination
Count multiplications for solving n×n system with LU

formulas.name: LU Factorisation (no pivoting)
formulas.latex: A = LU,\quad L \text{ unit lower triangular},\; U \text{ upper triangular}
formulas.plain: A = L * U, where L is unit lower-triangular (1s on diagonal) and U is upper-triangular
formulas.whenToUse: When A is square and Gaussian elimination succeeds without row exchanges

terms.means: n×n coefficient matrix
lower triangular matrix with 1s on main diagonal
upper triangular matrix (result of forward elimination)

formulas.trap: L has 1s on the diagonal (unit lower triangular), not arbitrary values — forgetting this causes errors when reading off L entries

formulas.name: LU with Partial Pivoting (PA = LU)
formulas.plain: P*A = L*U, where P is a permutation matrix recording row swaps
formulas.whenToUse: When row interchanges are needed for numerical stability or when A has a zero pivot

terms.means: permutation matrix encoding the row swaps
unit lower triangular factor after pivoting
upper triangular factor after pivoting

formulas.trap: Without pivoting a zero pivot causes division by zero; PA = LU always exists for any invertible A

formulas.name: Doolittle's Formula for L and U Entries
formulas.latex: u_{ij} = a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} l_{ik}\, u_{kj}, \quad l_{ij} = \frac{1}{u_{jj}}\!\left(a_{ij} - \sum_{k=1}^{j-1} l_{ik}\, u_{kj}\right)
formulas.whenToUse: Directly computing L and U entries without performing full Gaussian elimination steps

terms.means: entry of U in row i, column j
entry of L in row i, column j (i > j)
entry of original matrix A

formulas.trap: Row index i must satisfy i <= j for U entries; i > j for L entries; diagonal of L is always 1

formulas.name: Gaussian Elimination Multiplier (fills L)
formulas.plain: m[i][k] = a[i][k] / a[k][k]  (pivot row k, target row i)
formulas.whenToUse: During forward elimination; these multipliers are stored as the sub-diagonal entries of L

terms.means: multiplier used to eliminate entry in row i using pivot row k
pivot element at step k

formulas.trap: Multipliers are stored in L, not subtracted from L; the sign is positive in L even though they appear with minus signs during row reduction

formulas.name: Forward Substitution (Ly = b)
formulas.latex: y_i = b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} l_{ij}\, y_j, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
formulas.plain: y[i] = b[i] - sum_{j=1}^{i-1} L[i][j]*y[j]   (solve from top to bottom)
formulas.whenToUse: First step after LU factorisation: solve Ly = b to find intermediate vector y

terms.means: intermediate solution vector
right-hand side vector
sub-diagonal entry of L

formulas.trap: Since L is unit lower triangular (diagonal = 1), no division by diagonal is needed in forward substitution

formulas.name: Back Substitution (Ux = y)
formulas.latex: x_i = \frac{1}{u_{ii}}\!\left(y_i - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} u_{ij}\, x_j\right), \quad i = n, n{-}1, \ldots, 1
formulas.plain: x[i] = (y[i] - sum_{j=i+1}^{n} U[i][j]*x[j]) / U[i][i]   (solve from bottom to top)
formulas.whenToUse: Second step after forward substitution: solve Ux = y to find the actual solution x

terms.means: solution vector
diagonal entry of U (must be non-zero)

formulas.trap: If any u_{ii} = 0 the system is singular; back-substitution fails — check determinant first

formulas.name: Efficiency for Multiple Right-Hand Sides
formulas.plain: LU factorisation costs O(n^3/3) once; each subsequent solve costs O(n^2)
formulas.whenToUse: Solving Ax = b_1, Ax = b_2, ... Ax = b_k with the same A; reuse L and U

terms.means: number of distinct right-hand side vectors
dimension of the system

formulas.trap: Naive repeated Gaussian elimination costs O(k*n^3/3); LU makes it O(n^3/3 + k*n^2) — a huge saving for large k

formulas.name: Determinant from LU Factors
formulas.plain: det(A) = product of diagonal entries of U  (since det(L)=1 for unit lower triangular L)
formulas.whenToUse: Computing det(A) efficiently using the LU factorisation

terms.means: i-th diagonal entry of U
equals 1 because L is unit lower triangular

formulas.trap: With partial pivoting (PA = LU), det(A) = (-1)^s * prod(u_ii) where s = number of row swaps; missing the sign is a common error

formulas.name: Determinant with Pivoting Sign
formulas.plain: det(A) = (-1)^(number_of_row_swaps) * product of diagonal entries of U
formulas.whenToUse: When partial pivoting is used in PA = LU; the permutation P contributes a sign

terms.means: number of row interchanges during pivoting
sign from the permutation matrix P

formulas.trap: An even number of swaps keeps the sign positive; odd swaps flip the sign — always count swaps carefully

formulas.name: Matrix Inverse via LU
formulas.latex: A^{-1}[:,j] = U^{-1}L^{-1}e_j \quad\Leftrightarrow\quad \text{solve } Ax_j = e_j \text{ for } j=1,\ldots,n
formulas.plain: Each column of A^-1 is found by solving A*x_j = e_j using LU; do n forward+back substitutions
formulas.whenToUse: Computing the full inverse of A by applying LU solve n times (once per standard basis vector e_j)

terms.means: j-th standard basis vector (column of identity matrix)
j-th column of A^{-1}

formulas.trap: Computing the inverse costs O(n^3) total; avoid computing A^{-1} just to solve a single Ax = b — direct LU solve is cheaper

formulas.name: Existence Condition for LU (no pivoting)
formulas.latex: \text{LU exists (without pivoting)} \iff \text{all leading principal minors of } A \text{ are non-zero}
formulas.plain: LU decomposition (without pivoting) exists if and only if all leading principal minors det(A_k) != 0 for k=1,...,n
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether A can be factored as LU without any row exchanges

terms.means: k×k leading principal submatrix of A
determinant of the k×k top-left submatrix

formulas.trap: Invertibility (det(A)≠0) is necessary but NOT sufficient for LU without pivoting; a non-singular matrix may still need row swaps

formulas.name: PA = LU Always Exists for Invertible A
formulas.plain: For every invertible square matrix A, there exist permutation P, unit lower-triangular L, upper-triangular U such that PA = LU
formulas.whenToUse: When a problem states A is invertible and asks whether LU (with pivoting) exists

terms.means: permutation matrix (product of elementary row-swap matrices)

formulas.trap: PA = LU holds for all invertible A; the decomposition without P (bare LU) may fail even for invertible A

formulas.name: Uniqueness of LU (Doolittle convention)
formulas.latex: L \text{ unit lower triangular (}l_{ii}=1\text{)} \implies LU \text{ factorisation is unique if it exists}
formulas.plain: Fixing the diagonal of L to be all 1s (Doolittle) makes the LU factorisation unique
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether LU is unique — it is, under the Doolittle (or Crout) convention

terms.means: Doolittle convention: L has unit diagonal

formulas.trap: Without the convention of fixing L's diagonal (or U's), infinitely many LU pairs exist; in GATE problems Doolittle is assumed

formulas.name: Cholesky Decomposition (SPD matrices)
formulas.latex: A = LL^T,\quad A \text{ symmetric positive definite}
formulas.plain: A = L * L^T  (Cholesky), where L is lower triangular; valid only when A is symmetric positive definite
formulas.whenToUse: When A is symmetric and positive definite; Cholesky is twice as efficient as full LU

terms.means: lower triangular matrix with positive diagonal entries
transpose of L, serves as the upper triangular factor

formulas.trap: Cholesky fails if A is not positive definite; encountering a negative square root during computation indicates A is not SPD

formulas.name: LU of Tridiagonal Matrix (Thomas Algorithm)
formulas.plain: LU factorisation of a tridiagonal n×n matrix costs O(n) instead of O(n^3)
formulas.whenToUse: When A is tridiagonal (e.g. arising from finite difference discretisation of PDEs)

terms.means: linear cost exploiting the band structure

formulas.trap: Do not apply general O(n^3) LU to tridiagonal systems; recognise band structure and use Thomas algorithm

formulas.name: LDL^T Decomposition
formulas.plain: A = L * D * L^T, where D is diagonal and L is unit lower triangular
formulas.whenToUse: Symmetric (not necessarily SPD) matrices; avoids square roots unlike Cholesky

terms.means: diagonal matrix of pivots
unit lower triangular matrix

formulas.trap: LDL^T is distinct from Cholesky; D may have negative entries for indefinite symmetric A, unlike Cholesky which requires SPD

formulas.name: Flop Count for LU Factorisation
formulas.plain: LU factorisation of n×n dense matrix costs approximately 2n^3/3 floating point operations
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing cost of LU vs other methods; approximating computation time

terms.means: matrix dimension
dominant term for large n

formulas.trap: Some sources quote n^3/3 multiplications only (ignoring additions); total operations (multiply + add) is 2n^3/3

formulas.name: Flop Count for Forward/Back Substitution
formulas.plain: Each forward or back substitution (solving triangular system) costs O(n^2) flops
formulas.whenToUse: Estimating cost of solving Ly = b or Ux = y after factorisation is done

terms.means: dominant cost for each triangular solve

formulas.trap: Factorisation (O(n^3)) dominates solve (O(n^2)); for many right-hand sides the O(n^3) cost is paid once
